What is the new zoning actually building?

Cambridge rewrote its zoning in 2025. Explore the permits, the projects, the developers’ own filings and the tradeoffs — the data first, the argument clearly labeled — and decide for yourself.

The street we have. The scale that’s coming.

Nobody should need to read 400 pages of zoning text to see what it means. Drag the handle: a Cambridge residential street as it stands, against the scale of development the code now invites — real photographs, credited below.

● A Cambridge street · Avon Hill vs. Mass + Main · Central Square Real photographs · more pairs coming
The street we have
The scale now permitted
↔
Left: Avon Hill Street, 2020 — photo M2545 · CC BY 4.0 · Wikimedia Commons Right: Market Central (Mass + Main), Massachusetts Ave — photo Kenneth C. Zirkel · CC BY-SA 4.0 · Wikimedia Commons

Both sides are real, credited photographs — not renderings. Mass + Main was approved by special permit before the 2025 ordinance; it shows the scale of building the new code now permits along multifamily corridors citywide without that project-by-project review. Parcel-accurate before/afters for each filed project ship with the Cambridge 360 3D model. Want the developers’ own renderings? Open the filed plans →

Aerial: Elena Saporta, landscape architect
Case study · Mid-Cambridge

Six buildings. A few blocks.
What the market builds unguided.

Within months of the 2025 ordinance, developers assembled six occupied multifamily buildings in a few-block radius of Ellery Street. The plans follow the arithmetic: modest apartments out, premium microunits in — because that is the highest-margin product, and margin is the only instruction the current code gives. This isn’t an argument against developers. It’s the case for a city that writes family homes, affordability and mixed use into the program — instead of hoping the market volunteers them.

"Neighbors are being displaced and luxury microunits are slated to replace older, more modestly priced housing."— CCC press release, July 2026
A portrait of the city

This is what the zoning debate is about.

Every photograph here was chosen to carry one piece of the argument — follow each caption to the section it illustrates. All images are openly licensed and credited.

Sunday on Memorial Drive: the parkway becomes Riverbend Park — the shared civic space density depends on, and the open-space standard the code should require. Open space that counts → Photo 4300streetcar · CC BY 4.0 · Wikimedia Commons

Start with a question

Four questions every resident deserves answered.

This site explains in plain language. The Cambridge 360 dashboard — in development with the Urban Intelligence team — lets you inspect the evidence behind every answer, parcel by parcel.

01 · DEVELOPMENT

What is being removed — and what replaces it?

Demolitions, tree loss, and the buildings permitted in their place. Not what zoning theoretically allows — what's actually happening.

Explore development → 02 · PEOPLE

How many people are affected?

Tenants displaced — kept strictly separate from "future population capacity." Right-to-return, businesses, and jobs in affected space.

Explore displacement → 03 · MARKET

What rents and prices actually result?

Executed rents, not marketing projections. What "luxury microunits" really charge compared to the homes they replaced.

Explore the market → 04 · FISCAL & CIVIC

What does the city gain — and what does it cost?

Assessed value removed vs. created, one-time revenue vs. recurring service costs — and the civic capacity new residents will need.

Explore the tradeoffs →
The facts

What they say. What the data says.

Claims made for the ordinance, checked against the city's own numbers.

Zoning capacity is not construction.

Upzoning changes what is legal, not what gets built. Cambridge already beats its Envision housing goal at the current pace — while the ordinance's real, immediate products are demolition permits and land speculation. Judge the code by what it demolishes and builds, not by what it hypothetically allows.

Fix the codeWhere we stand

Good urban design isn't optional.
Cambridge's ordinance skipped it.

This is repair, not repeal. Urban planners and zoning experts point to proven practices the ordinance left out — the Citizens' Petition puts them back.

Building BetterWhere we stand

We're not against building.
We're for building better.

We are pro-housing — actively. Cambridge should add on the order of 14,000 new homes by 2030, and they should be real homes for every kind of household: studios and one-bedrooms, and the two-, three- and four-bedroom homes families with children need, built so seniors can age in the neighborhoods they made. Half of those homes should be genuinely affordable, none of the buildings taller than eight stories, and every large one mixed-use — shops, restaurants and community space at the street. Getting there is a matter of being smart about three things: where we develop, how we develop, and what we develop.

WHERE · 01

Build where it's empty

Surface parking lots, vacant commercial strips and underused industrial land near transit can hold thousands of homes — without demolishing anyone's. Our Atlas maps the exact lots: acres of them, a quarter-mile from stations. Density belongs where it displaces asphalt, not neighbors.

See the opportunity lots →
HOW · 02

Civic space, built in

A skating rink in the middle of lab-land proves it works: big projects can carry real shared space. The mechanisms exist — require community rooms and ground-floor civic uses in projects over a size threshold, set enforceable standards for privately-owned public spaces (hours, seating, sunlight), and let large developments fund the parks their residents will need.

Kendall Sq rink · Daderot · CC0How the code gets there →
WHAT · 03

Homes for every household

Count bedrooms, not just units. The 14,000 homes should come as a real mix — studios and one-bedrooms for singles and seniors, two-, three- and four-bedroom homes so families with children can stay in the city. Integrated, multigenerational, mixed-income housing — with the AHO's ~1,031 permanently affordable homes (29 at 4 Mellen St, applications open now) showing what commitment looks like.

The AHO pipeline, mapped →
HOW · 04

Ground floors that give back

Look at the base, not the tower: shops, a café, doors to the sidewalk. Whatever you think of its height, Mass+Main's ground floor gives the street more than it takes. Every large project can be required to do the same — active frontages, small-business space at survivable rents, stoops instead of blank walls.

Mass+Main · K. Zirkel · CC BY-SA 4.0Design standards →
HOW · 05

Streets for people

Every Sunday, Memorial Drive closes to cars and fills with people — proof that Cambridge loves a shared street. Growth should arrive with the transit, safe crossings and curb management that make car-light living actually work, sequenced together instead of years apart.

Riverbend Park · 4300streetcar · CC BY 4.0Sequencing growth →
WHERE · 06

Reclaim, don't raze

Danehy Park is fifty acres of playing fields built on the brickyard clay pits that built Boston. That's the Cambridge tradition worth continuing: transform the exhausted and underused — landfills, lots, rail yards — and let the loved places keep standing.

Danehy Park · Daderot · Public domainThe city's stories →
The memo: Building Better — smart about where, how and what we build. See where we’d build →Add your name
Past · Present · Future

The cornerstones of Cambridge — and what the zoning does to them.

Cambridge was assembled over generations: inherited streets, garden-city greenery, and civic spaces that make density livable. Each is exactly what the current code fails to protect.

Past — History

The inherited fabric

Beyond the two local historic districts and four conservation districts, most of Cambridge's older buildings are unprotected. The question every scenario must answer:

  • What is retained, adapted, or removed?
  • Full vs. partial demolition vs. adaptive reuse
  • Contributing buildings outside district lines
Present — Garden city

The living green structure

Canopy, permeable ground, shaded streets, park edges, sunlight, habitat. A civic value — and climate infrastructure.

  • Canopy lost, retained, and gained per project
  • Replacement saplings ≠ mature trees
  • Permeable ground vs. stormwater overflow
Future — Civic space

Capacity needs civic capacity

Squares, schools, libraries, sidewalks, transit — and the sunlight, shade, and wind conditions that make them usable.

  • New shadows on parks, yards & sidewalks
  • Demand on schools, transit & streets
  • Public investment needed per new resident
What greater legal capacity does not guarantee:
actual housing constructionaffordabilitypreservationadaptive reusegood architecturemature-canopy replacementimproved civic spacemunicipal investmenta positive fiscal result

Those outcomes depend on real projects, economics, phasing, design requirements, and public investment — which is precisely why the code needs standards, not just capacity.

CommunityWhere we stand

Density without community is just crowding.

A neighborhood is more than its housing count. It's who gets to stay, the shops that know your name, the places we gather, and the streets that connect us — and every one of them is under strain the ordinance never accounted for.

The streets we love
The people who stay
The corners that know your name
01

Multigenerational Cambridge

Elders aging in place, families near schools, students, and longtime tenants share these blocks — because modest apartments and triple-deckers make staying possible across generations. Teardown-for-luxury replaces that mix with a single price point, and the community that took a century to grow leaves in a single lease cycle.

Residents' concerns about protecting "living conditions attracting multigenerational inhabitants" have been raised as far as the district's congressional office.
02

The corner businesses

Neighborhood shops run on local regulars and survivable rents. When the blocks around them turn over to high-rent microunits — and construction closes sidewalks for years — the decades-old ice cream shop, hardware store, and café are collateral damage no spreadsheet counts.

Displaced businesses and jobs in affected space belong on the dashboard — never lumped into "development activity."
03

The places we gather

Libraries, parks, schoolyards, squares, sidewalks — civic space is where a city becomes a community. Ellery Street's teardowns sit steps from the Main Library and Joan Lorentz Park: new height means new shadow and wind on exactly the places neighbors actually share. The code counts balconies as "open space" while the real commons darkens.

Civic usability = sunlight + shade + wind + access — measurable, and unmeasured by the ordinance.
04

Streets that still work

"A nightmare to drive, bike, and walk" — that's how neighbors describe Cambridge already, before thousands of new units with zero required parking. Add Red Line shutdowns and 65% of Broadway's on-street parking removed, and the ordinance's traffic math simply isn't there.

Growth should be sequenced with transit and street capacity — an infrastructure trigger the code left out.

The hellscape of Cambridge traffic, transit, parking. It is now a nightmare to drive, bike and walk in Cambridge.

— Neighborhood post, Western Harvard Square · 71 reactions, 205 comments in a day

We need to build Affordable Housing the right way.

— Sara Nelson, CARE Housing Cambridge

What kind of Cambridge do we want? The Cambridge we love is worth protecting.

— CCC community campaign

Where this stands

The road to a better code.

The ordinance can be amended — that’s how zoning works. Here’s the path, and where your voice lands hardest.

Ordinance Committee — Tuesday, July 21 · 3:00 PM
Public comment is open. Two minutes of your voice outweighs a hundred pages of code.
Sign up to speak →
Take action

Add your voice. It's working.

Sign the petition

Tell the City Council to amend the ordinance: graduated heights, real setbacks, tree protection, and design review. Housing growth that fits Cambridge.

847 signaturesgoal 2,500

We never sell your data. One signature per person — signatures are verified before delivery to the Council. (Preview build: submissions are stored locally until the campaign API goes live.)

Fuel the campaign

Donations fund the mapping, analysis, printing, and outreach that make this ordinance's impact impossible to ignore. Processed securely via Donorbox through the Cambridge Citywide Coalition.

Stay awake

One sharp email before every vote.

No noise — a short brief on what's being decided, what the data says, and the one action that matters most that week.
